blocks.text: For he says, “At an acceptable time I have listened to you, and on a day of salvation I have helped you.” See, now is the acceptable time; see, now is the day of salvation!
We are putting no obstacle in anyone's way, so that no fault may be found with our ministry,
but as servants of God we have commended ourselves in every way: through great endurance, in afflictions, hardships, calamities,
4a : intensifies the theme of boasting, prominent in 1 Cor, using language of self-commendation and defense: cf. 3.1; 4.2; 5.12; 10.12; 10.18; 12.11.
beatings, imprisonments, riots, labors, sleepless nights, hunger;
4b–5 : A catalogue of afflictions followed by a catalogue of divinely empowered virtues (vv. 6–7).
by purity, knowledge, patience, kindness, holiness of spirit, genuine love,
truthful speech, and the power of God; with the weapons of righteousness for the right hand and for the left;
7 : , Paul often uses military metaphors, e.g., 2.14n.; 10.4–5; Phil 2.25; Philem 1.2; Gal 5.13,17; Rom 13.12; 1 Thess 5.8.
in honor and dishonor, in ill repute and good repute. We are treated as impostors, and yet are true;
as unknown, and yet are well known; as dying, and see—we are alive; as punished, and yet not killed;
as sorrowful, yet always rejoicing; as poor, yet making many rich; as having nothing, and yet possessing everything.
6.1–10 : Summary of defense and appeal to affection.

8–10 : A series of paired antitheses illustrates the paradoxical nature of apostolic work; see Gal 5.22–23; Phil 4.8; Col 3.12; 1 Pet 3.8; 2 Pet 1.5–7. In Stoic philosophy such lists describe the philosopher's self-sufficiency, but here support Paul's God-sourced self-defense.
We have spoken frankly to you Corinthians; our heart is wide open to you.
11 . , lit., “our mouth is open.”
There is no restriction in our affections, but only in yours.
12 : , lit., “the inner organs,” the seat of the emotions.
In return—I speak as to children—open wide your hearts also.
5.11–6.13: The ministry of reconciliation amid manifold afflictions.
Do not be mismatched with unbelievers. For what partnership is there between righteousness and lawlessness? Or what fellowship is there between light and darkness?

14 : , lit., “misyoked,” a term related to the injunction against interbreeding animals in Lev 19.19; it may relate to marriages (cf. 1 Cor 7.12–16), but also to business relations. (Rom 6.19); (Rom 2.19; 13.12; 1 Cor 4.5; 1 Thess 5.4–5).
What agreement does Christ have with Beliar? Or what does a believer share with an unbeliever?
15 : , variant of Belial (Heb “worthlessness”), is attested only here in biblical literature; more commonly “Satan” (see 2.11n.; 11.14; 12.7).
What agreement has the temple of God with idols? For we are the temple of the living God; as God said, “I will live in them and walk among them, and I will be their God, and they shall be my people.
16 : , see 1 Cor 3.16. , a chain of scriptural citations follows:
16d conflates Ezek 37.27 and Lev 26.12;
Therefore come out from them, and be separate from them, says the Lord, and touch nothing unclean; then I will welcome you,
17d : Ezek 20.34 (LXX);
and I will be your father, and you shall be my sons and daughters, says the Lord Almighty.”
1.12–7.16: Body, Part I: Visitation, trauma, reconciliation.
6.14–7.1: Warnings about purity. These verses insert an uncharacteristic theme into the discourse on affection that resumes at 7.2.
